codeReview.summary: Commit `cf8618f` correctly replaces the nine deep imports introduced by `b6b1367` with `@parchi/shared` entrypoint imports across the profile and provider-instance suites, and `packages/shared/src/index.ts` exports the required symbols. That fixes the deep-import defect itself, but the rerun still fails scrutiny overall because it again bundles unrelated version bumps in `package.json` and both extension manifests, repeating the same scope violation already called out on the prior `fix-test-file-line-limits` review instead of clearing it.

issues.description: The rerun commit bumps the package version from `0.4.63` to `0.4.64` even though this feature only changes test imports. Mission AGENTS forbids bundling version changes with non-release refactor/fix work, so this repeats the prior feature's scope violation rather than resolving it.

The Chrome manifest version is also bumped from `0.4.63` to `0.4.64` in the same test-import fix commit. This release-metadata edit is unrelated to replacing deep imports and remains a blocking scope-discipline issue.

The Firefox manifest version bump from `0.4.63` to `0.4.64` repeats the same forbidden metadata churn. Because the reviewed feature scope is limited to public-entrypoint test imports, this remains a blocking scrutiny failure.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The `refactor-worker` completion gate is still not being enforced consistently. The skill requires `npm run check:repo-standards` and says `followedProcedure` may only be true when all validation steps were run and passed, but this worker's transcript shows `check:repo-standards` failed while the handoff omits that command and still records `followedProcedure: true`.

summary: The rerun feature fixes the deep shared imports themselves: the nine affected profile/provider-instance tests now use `@parchi/shared`, and no `packages/shared/src` imports remain in those suites. Review still fails because commit `cf8618f` again includes out-of-scope version bumps in `package.json` plus both manifests, so the broader scope-discipline failure from the prior review is still not resolved.
